title: Testing Superpositions of Detector Trajectories
authors: Cisco Gooding, Taylor Cey, Robert Mann
abstract: We propose a realizable experiment to test the response of a particle detector prepared in a superposition of locations interacting with a relativistic quantum field. Using a beamsplitter to prepare two superposed branches of a modulated laser probe, these branches are directed to intersect a pancake-shaped Bose-Einstein condensate at two separate locations. The branches are then recombined with another beamsplitter. Heterodyning one of the outputs, the response function corresponding to an Unruh-deWitt detector in a superposition of locations interacting with a (2+1)-dimensional massless scalar field is shown to appear in the difference photocurrent power spectrum. Operating beyond the standard quantum limit using squeezed light, we estimate the signal-to-noise ratio $SNR\gtrsim 10$ for extracting the response function over a broad set of baseband frequencies.
summary: The paper proposes an experimental setup using a Bose-Einstein Condensate to test the response of a particle detector in a superposition of locations. By using a modulated laser probe and heterodyne detection, the authors show it is possible to isolate the interference signal from the detector's trajectory. The study demonstrates that using squeezed light can significantly enhance the signal-to-noise ratio for this measurement.
main_problem: Proposing a realizable experiment to detect and witness the superposition of detector trajectories by measuring the response of an Unruh-DeWitt detector to a relativistic quantum field.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the interference signal (off-diagonal terms) can be isolated via heterodyne detection of a laser probe, achieving a signal-to-noise ratio of SNR >= 10 using squeezed light.
method: The method involves using a modulated laser probe split into two branches that intersect a Bose-Einstein Condensate at two locations, followed by recombination and heterodyne detection to extract the difference-photocurrent power spectral density.
model_or_system: An analogue system using a pancake-shaped Bose-Einstein Condensate (BEC) of 133Cs atoms to simulate a (2+1)-dimensional massless scalar field, with an Unruh-DeWitte detector model interacting with density fluctuations.
key_observables: The difference-photocurrent power spectral density and the total response function F(nu) containing the interference term.
important_parameters: Energy gap nu, coupling constant lambda, displacement delta, and the use of squeezed light to operate beyond the standard quantum limit.
main_assumptions: Assumes the ability to implement specific parameter regimes in a 133Cs BEC and requires sufficient laser power for nondestructive continuous measurement.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a schematic of the experimental setup, including the EOM, BEC interaction points, beamsplitter recombination, and the heterodyne detection components.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the theoretical Unruh-DeWitt model, maps it to a BEC analogue system, details the experimental detection scheme using laser probes, calculates the expected signal and noise, and estimates the achievable SNR.
why_interesting: It bridges quantum field theory (Unruh effect) with experimental AMO physics, providing a concrete way to use BECs and quantum optics (squeezed light) to probe fundamental relativistic quantum phenomena.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental schematic for probing BEC density fluctuations from a superposition of detector locations sepa- rated by δ = |x1 −x2|. Prior to interaction, an electro-optic modulator (EOM) and a filter are used to prepare the mod- ulated laser field (17). Post-interaction, the laser probes are recombined in a beamsplitter, and the “sum” output E+ is heterodyned using a reference beam ELO, yielding the desired response function (25). An acousto-optic modulator (AOM) and a phase-shifter (PS) are incorporated in the reference beam to facilitate the heterodyne process [11].
